message:
feat(tui): add ChartDataProvider trait and time-series aggregation infrastructure (br-1k84y)

Implements the foundational data pipeline for converting raw EventRingBuffer
events into chart-ready time-series data. Adds Granularity enum (1s-5m buckets),
AggregatedTimeSeries struct with incremental refresh via sequence tracking, and
the ChartDataProvider trait consumed by downstream chart widgets.

Concrete providers: ThroughputProvider (calls/sec), LatencyProvider (P50/P95/P99),
ResourceProvider (DB stats from HealthPulse), EventHeatmapProvider (per-kind counts
with Canvas heatmap grid output). All 101 tui_widgets tests pass.

Closes br-1k84y. Unblocks br-1f7td, br-3q8v0, br-333hh, br-18wct, br-edpom.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

## What to build
Enable independent gates to run concurrently via a --parallel flag. This is a major
improvement over ci.sh which runs all 13 gates sequentially.

## Parallelization strategy
Gates are grouped by parallel_group in GateConfig. Gates in the same group run
sequentially (they may share resources), but different groups run concurrently.

Default groups:
- Group "compile": fmt check, clippy, build workspace (share compiler state)
- Group "test": unit+integration tests, mode matrix, semantic conformance, perf regressions, help snapshots (share test binaries)
- Group "docs": release docs presence check (standalone)
- Group "e2e": all E2E gates (share server resources, run sequentially within group)

In --parallel mode, "compile" runs first (other groups depend on build artifacts),
then "test", "docs", and "e2e" run concurrently.

## Implementation notes
- Use std::thread::spawn or scoped threads for parallelism (avoid async complexity)
- Each gate still gets its own Command subprocess, so isolation is natural
- Aggregate results after all threads complete
- Output interleaving: prefix each line with [gate_name] to distinguish parallel output
- Total elapsed time should be wall-clock (not sum of individual gate times)
- Without --parallel, gates run sequentially in definition order (backward compatible)

## Considerations
- cargo test already parallelizes internally, so running two test groups in parallel
  may cause resource contention. The parallel_group mechanism prevents this.
- The compile group MUST complete before test group starts (test binaries need to exist)

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/ci.rs (enhance run_gates with parallel logic)

IMPORTANT: The bash script ci.sh does NOT have a --parallel flag.
Running gates in parallel is a NEW FEATURE being added in the Rust port.

This bead should be clearly labeled as an ENHANCEMENT, not a port.

The existing bash runs all gates sequentially in a single process.
The Rust implementation should:
1. First implement sequential execution (matching bash parity in T1.2)
2. Then add --parallel as an enhancement (this bead T1.5)

Parallelism constraints:
- Gates within the same category CAN run in parallel (they don't interact)
- Gates across categories CAN also run in parallel
- BUT: stdout/stderr must be captured per-gate (not interleaved)
- The NDJSON sidecar should still reflect execution order for reproducibility
- The --report JSON should have the same structure regardless of parallel/sequential

Note: This is a strictly additive feature. If it proves too complex,
it can be deferred without affecting parity.

T1.6 should include tests for:

These are ENHANCEMENT beads — the core test suite (T1.7) validates

events into chart-ready time-series data.

DESIGN:
```rust
pub trait ChartDataProvider {
    fn series_count(&self) -> usize;
    fn series_label(&self, idx: usize) -> &str;
    fn data_points(&self, idx: usize, window: Duration) -> &[(f64, f64)]; // (timestamp, value)
    fn y_range(&self) -> (f64, f64);
}
```

Concrete implementations:
- ThroughputProvider: messages/sec from ToolCallEnd events, 1-second buckets
- LatencyProvider: per-tool P50/P95/P99 from ToolCallEnd events
- ResourceProvider: memory/CPU from HealthPulse events
- EventHeatmapProvider: event-type counts per time bucket for Canvas rendering

AGGREGATION STRATEGY:
- Maintain rolling windows at multiple granularities (1s, 5s, 30s, 1m, 5m)
- Use a single pass over EventRingBuffer with events_since_seq() for incremental updates
- Cache aggregated data in AggregatedTimeSeries struct (timestamp, values per series)
- Invalidate cache on new data arrival (sequence number tracking)

This is foundational infrastructure — all chart widgets in T1.2-T1.5 depend on it.

## What to build
Given a failure artifact JSON file, re-run the failed test with the same seed/environment
to reproduce the failure. Replaces the reproduce mode in flake_triage.sh.

## Current bash behavior (lines ~30-50 of flake_triage.sh)
```bash
test_name=$(python3 -c "import json; print(json.load(open('$artifact')).get('test_name',''))")
seed=$(python3 -c "import json; print(json.load(open('$artifact')).get('seed',''))")
cargo test "$test_name" -- --seed "$seed"
```

## Rust implementation
```rust
struct ReproductionConfig {
    artifact_path: PathBuf,
    verbose: bool,         // pass --nocapture to cargo test
    timeout: Duration,
}

struct ReproductionResult {
    test_name: String,
    seed: Option<u64>,
    reproduced: bool,      // true if the test failed again
    exit_code: i32,
    stdout: String,
    stderr: String,
    elapsed: Duration,
}

fn reproduce_failure(config: &ReproductionConfig) -> Result<ReproductionResult, Error>
```

## Implementation notes
- Parse the artifact JSON to extract test_name, seed, and any environment variables
- Reconstruct the cargo test command with the same parameters
- Use Command::output() to capture full stdout/stderr (useful for debugging)
- If the test passes (not reproduced), report that clearly — it may be an environment-
  dependent failure

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/flake_triage.rs

 {"id":"br-3nr5u","title":"Track B: Evidence Ledger Schema","description":"**Background**

The policy/threshold/adaptive domain has 2670 signal hits across the codebase, but all decision-making is currently ad-hoc: backpressure thresholds are hardcoded constants, TUI rendering strategies use magic numbers, cache TTLs are fixed. There is no structured record of why a particular decision was made, what the inputs were, or how the outcome compared to the prior.

**Graveyard reference:** Section 0.19 (Evidence Ledger). An evidence ledger is a structured, append-only log of decisions made under uncertainty. Each entry records: (1) the decision point identifier, (2) the state/evidence at decision time, (3) the action chosen, (4) the expected outcome, (5) the actual outcome (filled in later). This enables: Bayesian updating of priors, automated A/B testing, drift detection, and operator transparency.

**EV calculation:** (severity=4 * breadth=5 * feasibility=5) / (risk=2 * effort=2) = 25.0

This track is purely additive -- it does not change any existing behavior. It adds telemetry infrastructure that all subsequent tracks (D, G, H) depend on.","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:

 {"id":"br-3o7hh","title":"R4.3: Implement am robot health — system diagnostics synthesis (probes, DB pool, disk, anomalies)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:17:19.140240211Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T05:21:46.268788357Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T05:21:46.268764352Z","close_reason":"Implemented: robot health with 5 probes (db_connectivity, circuit_breakers, backpressure, integrity, disk), per-circuit status, alerts and actions","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","depends_on_id":"br-1nmyh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:20:54.350093017Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":315,"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.3: `am robot health`

## What
System health synthesis — equivalent to the SystemHealth TUI screen. Includes connection probes, diagnostics, and remediation hints.

## Data Collection Strategy
The CLI doesn't have a running server to probe. Instead:
1. **DB probe**: Open sync SQLite connection, measure latency
2. **Disk probe**: Check storage root exists, measure DB file size, archive size
3. **Process probe**: Check if mcp-agent-mail server is running on expected port
4. **Tool discovery**: Count tools available (from the tool registry in code)

## Implementation
```rust
fn robot_health(db_path: &str, storage_root: &str) -> HealthData {
    let probes = vec![];

    // DB probe
    let start = Instant::now();
    let db = open_db_sync(db_path);
    let db_latency = start.elapsed().as_millis();
    probes.push(HealthProbe { name: "database", status: "ok", latency_ms: db_latency, detail: None });

    // Disk probe
    let db_size = fs::metadata(db_path).map(|m| m.len()).unwrap_or(0);
    let archive_size = dir_size(storage_root);

    // TCP probe (check if server is running)
    let tcp_ok = TcpStream::connect_timeout(&addr, Duration::from_secs(1)).is_ok();

    // HTTP probe (if TCP ok, try unauthenticated request)
    if tcp_ok {
        // curl equivalent: GET /mcp/ → expect 401
    }
}
```

## Output Format
```
status: ok
health_level: healthy

probes:
  database: ok (3ms)
  disk: ok (db=12.4MB, archive=45.2MB)
  tcp: ok (port 8765 reachable)
  http: ok (401 Unauthorized — auth working)
  tool_count: 34

database:
  path: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail/agent_mail.db
  size_mb: 12.4
  wal_size_mb: 0.1

disk:
  storage_root: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail
  archive_size_mb: 45.2
  free_space_gb: 120.5

anomalies[0]:

_actions[0]:
```

## Health Level Classification
- **healthy**: All probes ok
- **degraded**: Some probes failed but DB accessible
- **error**: DB unreachable or critical failure

## Acceptance Criteria
- All probe types implemented
- Latency measured for DB probe
- Disk sizes reported in human-readable format
- Server running/not-running both handled gracefully
- Health level correctly derived from probe results
","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:28:25Z"},{"id":340,"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.3: `am robot health`

## What
System health synthesis — equivalent to the SystemHealth TUI screen. Includes connection probes, diagnostics, and remediation hints.

## Data Collection Strategy
The CLI doesn't have a running server to probe. Instead:
1. **DB probe**: Open sync SQLite connection, measure latency
2. **Disk probe**: Check storage root exists, measure DB file size, archive size
3. **Process probe**: Check if mcp-agent-mail server is running on expected port
4. **Tool discovery**: Count tools available (from the tool registry in code)

## Implementation
```rust
fn robot_health(db_path: &str, storage_root: &str) -> HealthData {
    let probes = vec![];

    // DB probe
    let start = Instant::now();
    let db = open_db_sync(db_path);
    let db_latency = start.elapsed().as_millis();
    probes.push(HealthProbe { name: "database", status: "ok", latency_ms: db_latency, detail: None });

    // Disk probe
    let db_size = fs::metadata(db_path).map(|m| m.len()).unwrap_or(0);
    let archive_size = dir_size(storage_root);

    // TCP probe (check if server is running)
    let tcp_ok = TcpStream::connect_timeout(&addr, Duration::from_secs(1)).is_ok();

    // HTTP probe (if TCP ok, try unauthenticated request)
    if tcp_ok {
        // curl equivalent: GET /mcp/ → expect 401
    }
}
```

## Output Format
```
status: ok
health_level: healthy

probes:
  database: ok (3ms)
  disk: ok (db=12.4MB, archive=45.2MB)
  tcp: ok (port 8765 reachable)
  http: ok (401 Unauthorized — auth working)
  tool_count: 34

database:
  path: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail/agent_mail.db
  size_mb: 12.4
  wal_size_mb: 0.1

disk:
  storage_root: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail
  archive_size_mb: 45.2
  free_space_gb: 120.5

anomalies[0]:

_actions[0]:
```

## Health Level Classification
- **healthy**: All probes ok
- **degraded**: Some probes failed but DB accessible
- **error**: DB unreachable or critical failure

## Acceptance Criteria
- All probe types implemented
- Latency measured for DB probe
- Disk sizes reported in human-readable format
- Server running/not-running both handled gracefully
- Health level correctly derived from probe results

-{"id":"br-3ocb0","title":"I.2: SQL template caching with OnceLock","description":"**Background**

In `crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs`, several hot-path functions build SQL strings using `format!()` on every call (e.g., line 3840-3843, 3897-3903, 3912-3917). The SQL template structure is stable -- only the number of placeholders varies. For fixed-arity queries, the template can be computed once and reused.

The string cloning issue in `run_like_fallback` (line 2197-2199) is also addressed here:
```rust
let escaped = format!("%{}%", like_escape(term));
params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()));  // unnecessary clone
params.push(Value::Text(escaped));
```

**Scope / Adoption wedge**

1. **OnceLock templates for fixed-arity queries**: For queries where the number of placeholders is known at compile time (e.g., `list_approved_contact_agent_ids` with a fixed IN clause), cache the SQL string in a `static` `OnceLock<String>`. Example:
   ```rust
   static APPROVED_CONTACTS_SQL: OnceLock<String> = OnceLock::new();
   fn approved_contacts_sql() -> &'static str {
       APPROVED_CONTACTS_SQL.get_or_init(|| {
           "SELECT b_agent_id FROM agent_links \
            WHERE a_project_id = ? AND a_agent_id = ? AND b_project_id = ? \
              AND status = 'approved' AND b_agent_id IN (?)"
           .to_string()
       })
   }
   ```
   Note: For variable-arity IN clauses, cache templates per arity (up to MAX_IN_CLAUSE_ITEMS).

2. **Eliminate string clone in LIKE fallback**: In `run_like_fallback` (line 2197-2199), the `escaped` string is cloned for the second parameter push. Instead, push the same format twice:
   ```rust
   let escaped = format!("%{}%", like_escape(term));
   params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()));
   params.push(Value::Text(escaped));
   ```
   This is already the pattern but can be optimized by using `Arc<str>` or by computing the two params separately. The simplest fix: keep the clone but wrap in `Value::Text` which likely already clones internally. Actually, looking at the code again: the `escaped.clone()` followed by `Value::Text(escaped)` is correct Rust -- the clone creates the first copy, then the original moves into the second. The "unnecessary clone" is actually necessary because two `Value::Text` instances are needed. The real optimization is to avoid the `format!` entirely for repeated terms.

   Better approach: For the WHERE clause building in LIKE fallback, pre-format the like pattern once and reference it:
   ```rust
   let patterns: Vec<String> = terms.iter().map(|t| format!("%{}%", like_escape(t))).collect();
   for pattern in &patterns {
       params.push(Value::Text(pattern.clone()));
       params.push(Value::Text(pattern.clone()));
   }
   ```
   This is the same number of clones but clearer intent.

3. **Template caching for variable-arity IN clauses**: Create a `PlaceholderCache` that stores precomputed placeholder strings for common arities:
   ```rust
   static PLACEHOLDER_CACHE: OnceLock<[String; 64]> = OnceLock::new();
   fn cached_placeholders(n: usize) -> &'static str {
       let cache = PLACEHOLDER_CACHE.get_or_init(|| {
           std::array::from_fn(|i| placeholders(i + 1))
       });
       if n > 0 && n <= 64 { &cache[n - 1] } else { /* fall back to dynamic */ }
   }
   ```

**Risks / Safe Mode**

- Risk: OnceLock initialization race (benign -- OnceLock handles this).
- Risk: Placeholder cache for arities > 64 falls back to dynamic allocation. This is the same as current behavior.
- Fallback trigger: None (pure optimization, no behavior change).

**Validation / Isomorphism proof**

- For every cached SQL template, `cached_template == format!(...)` for the same inputs. Verify by comparing in a test.
- For every cached placeholder string, `cached_placeholders(n) == placeholders(n)`. Verify by property test.

**Tests (5 required)**

1. `placeholder_cache_matches_dynamic` -- for n in 1..64, verify cached == dynamic
2. `sql_template_cache_consistent` -- verify OnceLock template matches format! output
3. `like_fallback_no_extra_clone` -- verify LIKE patterns are computed once per term
4. `template_cache_thread_safe` -- 10 threads access cache concurrently, no panic
5. `placeholder_cache_overflow_fallback` -- n=100 falls back to dynamic correctly","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- OnceLock SQL templates and placeholder cache are implemented for fixed-arity hot paths
- Unit tests validate template generation, placeholder correctness, and LIKE pattern edge cases
- Integration tests verify query outputs and ordering remain unchanged after caching
- E2E regression scenario confirms search/contact flows behave identically with optimization enabled
- Performance benchmarks show lower allocation/formatting overhead on hot query paths
- Diagnostics include cache hit/miss for templates and fallback generation counts","notes":"## Plan-Space Review Findings (2026-02-13)

**CONCERN: Sprawling scope — 3 things in 1 bead.** Items (1) OnceLock templates and (3) PlaceholderCache are related but (2) LIKE optimization is separate. The LIKE section is confused — it starts by calling the clone "unnecessary", then walks it back ("actually necessary"), then proposes a pre-format approach with "the same number of clones." Cut the LIKE section or rewrite it clearly.

**CONCERN: LOW user benefit.** The `format!()` cost for SQL templates is negligible compared to actual SQL execution time (100x-1000x slower). The `PlaceholderCache` is a micro-optimization. OnceLock for static queries is fine but measurable impact is near zero. Consider deprioritizing to P3.

**FIX: LIKE clone IS necessary.** Lines 2197-2199: `let escaped = format!(...)` then `params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()))` then `params.push(Value::Text(escaped))`. Two `Value::Text` need two owned Strings. The clone is necessary. Remove the "unnecessary clone" framing.

**FIX: Add a benchmark.** This is a perf bead with no performance measurement. Benchmark `placeholders()` and `format!()` costs to validate the optimization is worth the complexity.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:46:45.196698361Z","updated_at":"2026-02-14T00:42:35.256070409Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","perf"]}

 {"id":"br-3p7th","title":"R4.2: Implement am robot metrics — tool performance summary (calls, errors, error%, latency percentiles)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:17:18.912768416Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T05:16:40.375602663Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T05:16:40.375582285Z","close_reason":"Implemented: R4.2 robot metrics with per-tool calls/errors/latency, alert generation for high error rates and slow tools","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3p7th","depends_on_id":"br-1nmyh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:20:54.114144327Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":314,"issue_id":"br-3p7th","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.2: `am robot metrics`

## What
Tool performance summary from the query tracking system. Shows agents which tools are slow, erroring, or heavily used. Uses the existing `tool_metrics_snapshot_full()` function that powers the TUI Tool Metrics screen.

## Data Source
Call `tool_metrics_snapshot_full()` which returns per-tool metrics:
- call_count, error_count, avg_latency_ms, p95_ms, p99_ms
- These are computed from the global QueryTracker singleton

## Output Format
```
summary:
  total_calls: 1234
  total_errors: 12
  error_rate: 0.97%
  avg_latency_ms: 45

tools[10]{name,calls,errors,error_pct,avg_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms}:
  fetch_inbox,450,0,0.0,12,25,45
  send_message,200,3,1.5,89,150,200
  search_messages,180,1,0.6,120,250,400
  file_reservation_paths,150,0,0.0,8,15,20
  acknowledge_message,100,0,0.0,5,10,15
  register_agent,50,2,4.0,30,50,80
  health_check,40,0,0.0,3,5,8
  summarize_thread,30,5,16.7,2500,4000,5000
  macro_start_session,20,1,5.0,150,300,500
  whois,14,0,0.0,6,10,15

_alerts[1]{severity,summary}:
  warn,summarize_thread has 16.7% error rate (5/30 calls)
```

## Alert Generation
- Tool with error_pct > 10% → warn alert
- Tool with avg_ms > 2000 → warn alert (slow tool)
- Tool with error_pct > 50% → error alert
- Overall error_rate > 5% → error alert

## Sorting
Default: sort by call count descending (most-used first).
Can be changed to sort by error rate or latency via --sort flag (future enhancement).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Summary section aggregates totals correctly
- Per-tool metrics match actual tracker data
- Alert thresholds trigger correctly
- Percentile calculation correct (matches existing p95/p99 logic)
- Works when metrics are empty (no tools called yet)

-{"id":"br-3pns5","title":"I.3: Contact query UNION refactor","description":"**Background**

The `list_recent_contact_agent_ids()` function in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs` (lines 3873-3955) executes two separate queries sequentially:

1. **Sent query** (line 3897-3903): `SELECT DISTINCT r.agent_id FROM message_recipients r JOIN messages m ... WHERE m.sender_id = ? AND r.agent_id IN (?)`
2. **Received query** (line 3912-3917): `SELECT DISTINCT m.sender_id FROM messages m JOIN message_recipients r ... WHERE r.agent_id = ? AND m.sender_id IN (?)`

These two queries can be combined into a single `UNION` query, eliminating one DB round-trip and one connection acquisition:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT agent_id FROM (
    SELECT r.agent_id
    FROM message_recipients r
    JOIN messages m ON m.id = r.message_id
    WHERE m.project_id = ? AND m.sender_id = ? AND m.created_ts > ?
      AND r.agent_id IN (?,?,?)
    UNION
    SELECT m.sender_id
    FROM messages m
    JOIN message_recipients r ON r.message_id = m.id
    WHERE m.project_id = ? AND r.agent_id = ? AND m.created_ts > ?
      AND m.sender_id IN (?,?,?)
) sub
```

**Scope / Adoption wedge**

1. Replace the two separate queries with a single UNION query in `list_recent_contact_agent_ids()`.
2. The parameter vector must include the parameters for both branches of the UNION.
3. The result set naturally deduplicates (UNION, not UNION ALL), so the `sort_unstable(); dedup();` post-processing (lines 3947-3948) can be simplified to just extracting the ids.
4. The connection is acquired once (already the case -- the function uses `acquire_conn` once at line 3886).

**Implementation notes:**
- The UNION query has 6 fixed params (project_id, sender_id, since_ts for each branch) plus 2 * len(candidate_ids) for the two IN clauses.
- The `placeholders()` function already generates the right number of `?` markers.
- The column alias must be consistent across both branches: both select `agent_id` (rename `sender_id` to `agent_id` in the second branch).

**Risks / Safe Mode**

- Risk: UNION query plan may be worse than two separate queries. Mitigation: SQLite's query planner handles UNION well for this pattern. If EXPLAIN shows a full table scan, add an index on `(project_id, sender_id, created_ts)` for messages and `(message_id, agent_id)` for message_recipients (likely already exist).
- Risk: Parameter ordering error in the combined query. Mitigation: Write a comprehensive test with known data.
- Fallback trigger: If any existing contact-related test fails, revert.

**Validation / Isomorphism proof**

For any set of inputs (project_id, sender_id, candidate_ids, since_ts):
- `union_query(inputs) == old_two_queries(inputs)` (set equality of returned ids)
- Verify by running both on the same database with known test data and comparing results.

**Tests (5 required)**

1. `contact_union_matches_two_queries` -- insert known messages, run both old (two queries) and new (UNION), verify identical results
2. `contact_union_empty_candidates` -- empty candidate_ids returns empty vec (fast path)
3. `contact_union_no_results` -- no messages match criteria, returns empty
4. `contact_union_dedup_correct` -- agent appears in both sent and received, only appears once in result
5. `contact_union_large_candidate_list` -- 100 candidate ids, verify correct placeholders and results","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- Contact lookup is refactored to single UNION query with correct parameter binding and dedup semantics
- Unit tests cover ordering, dedup, empty-set, and mixed-contact edge cases
- Integration tests compare UNION results against legacy two-query baseline for parity
- E2E regression scenario validates contact views/policies remain user-identical
- Isomorphism check is automated and fails with actionable row-level diffs on mismatch
- Diagnostics include query timing, result cardinality, and dedup behavior counters","notes":"## Plan-Space Review Findings (2026-02-13)

**FIX: MAX_IN_CLAUSE_ITEMS double-use.** In the UNION query, the IN clause appears twice (once per branch). If `MAX_IN_CLAUSE_ITEMS = 999`, the UNION uses `2 * candidate_count` parameters. Verify this stays within SQLite's `SQLITE_MAX_VARIABLE_NUMBER` (default 32766). With our current cap, 2*999 = 1998 — well within limits.

**FIX: Column alias test.** The `get_named("sender_id")` to `get_named("agent_id")` rename in the UNION query's second branch must be tested explicitly. Verify the column alias works correctly with `Row::get_named`.

**Additional test:**

 {"id":"br-3qf9w","title":"R1.5: Add --format global flag to CLI arg parser with auto-detection (robot→toon, tty→table, pipe→json)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:16:48.669209352Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T04:47:21.350209269Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T04:47:21.350190795Z","close_reason":"resolve_format() auto-detection + is_prose_command() + IsTerminal detection in robot.rs with 4 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3qf9w","depends_on_id":"br-3i7fz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:17:47.556707184Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":300,"issue_id":"br-3qf9w","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R1.5: --format Global Flag + Auto-Detection

## What
Add `--format <toon|json|md>` as a global CLI flag for robot commands, with smart auto-detection when not specified.

## Where
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs` — in the Robot command definition (or global args)

## Auto-Detection Logic
```
if --format explicitly provided:
    use that format
else if command is thread/message:
    default to Markdown (prose-heavy content)
else if stdout is a TTY:
    default to TOON (human at terminal, but token-efficient)
else:
    default to JSON (piped output, machine-readable)
```

The TTY check uses `std::io::stdout().is_terminal()` (Rust 1.70+ stable API via `IsTerminal` trait).

## Why This Matters for Agents
- Agents NEVER run in a TTY (they pipe stdout) → auto-get JSON unless they ask for TOON
- Agents that know about TOON can explicitly pass `--format toon` for 40-60% token savings
- Humans at a terminal get TOON by default (readable but compact)
- Thread/message commands default to Markdown because conversations are prose

## Implementation Notes
- Add `--format` to the Robot subcommand enum, not as a global flag
  (only robot commands support format selection)
- The format arg should be `Option<OutputFormat>` — None triggers auto-detection
- Auto-detection runs once at dispatch time, result passed to all command handlers

## Acceptance Criteria
- `am robot status --format json` produces JSON
- `am robot status --format toon` produces TOON
- `am robot thread 123 --format md` produces Markdown
- Without --format: piped output → JSON, TTY → TOON, thread/message → Markdown
- Unit test for auto-detection logic with mocked is_terminal
